coredata.dc:title: High-performance NO2 gas sensor enabled by Fe, N co-doped GQDs modification and pulse-driven temperature modulation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Jiayin
Zhou, Weirong
Kong, Dehao
Gao, Yubing
Gao, Yuan
Wang, Yong
Lu, Geyu
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen dioxide (NO2) is a typical reactive nitrogen species harmful to health and the environment. However, NO2 sensors still suffer from low sensitivity and poor response/recovery rates. Herein, 3D In2O3 assembled by stacked nanosheets were prepared and modified with N element-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) and Fe, N co-doped GQDs (Fe,N-GQDs), respectively. The results of NO2-sensing performance indicated that the optimal sample (0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3) showed a high response of 414.5 to 1 ppm NO2 and could detect as low as 10 ppb at 50℃, which was 1.3 and 4.0 times higher than that of 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3 and In2O3, respectively. The gas-sensing kinetics were analyzed by establishing gas adsorption/desorption isotherms. The enhanced NO2 sensing properties of 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 were mainly due to the enhanced active site accessibility of stacked nanosheets with open layer spaces, the heterointerface between Fe,N-GQDs and In2O3, and electrical modulation of Fe,N-GQDs. Additionally, A pulse-driving circuit was designed for a pulse temperature modulation (PTM) strategy to further boost NO2 detection. The sensor response is further enhanced by 1.7 times, and the response/recovery time is reduced by 42.5 %/60.1 %. The NO2 concentration in vehicle exhaust was determined using PTM mode with good spike recoveries. It validated the reliability of the sensor for the quantitative detection of NO2 in the real environment. This work provides insights into sensor design and has the potential to contribute to the development of novel sensing platforms for other gas pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NO2 sensor
Trace detection
Heterojunction interface
Fe
N co-doped graphene quantum dots
In2O3 nanosheets

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of In2O3 and GQDs/In2O3 nanocomposites
Detection of NO2 in environmental vehicle exhaust

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of In2O3, Fe,N-GQDs and Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 preparation; (b) Conventional isothermal and (c) PTM module.
SEM, TEM and HRTEM of (a, d) In2O3; (b, e) 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3; (c, f) 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3; (g) EDS patterns of 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3.

(a) XRD patterns; (b) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and (c) BJH pore size distributions of In2O3, 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3 and 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3; (d) UV–vis and (e) (αhυ)2 vs photon energy (hυ) spectra; (f) Mott-Schottky plots of In2O3, 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3 and 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3.
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectra (a) survey scan; (b) C 1 s; (c) In 3d; (d) O 1 s; (e) N 1 s and (f) Fe 2p.

Temperature-dependent responses to 1 ppm NO2 of the sensors based on (a) In2O3, 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3, 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 and (b) In2O3, Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3; (c) the baseline resistance of all sensors; (d-h) concentration gradient response-recovery curves; (i) responses under different concentration gradients.

Non-linear fitting (red line) of experimental response part of the concentration-dependent resistance transients measured at 50°C (a) In2O3; (b) 0.3 w% N-GQDs/In2O3; (c) 0.3 w% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3; (d) 1/τres vs concentration plots; (e) In (τres) vs In (C) plots; (f) Dynamic response/recovery curves and (g) response/recovery time; (h) dynamic response/recovery curves and (i) response/recovery time of In2O3 and Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 to 1 ppm NO2 at 50℃.

(a) Repeated response-recovery curves; (b) the response of In2O3, 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3 and 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 to 1 ppm NO2 within 30 days at 50℃; (c) normalized response of the three sensors as a function of relative humidity (20–80 %, 25℃) at 50℃; (d) responses to various gases at 50℃.

0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 (a) Schematic illustration of a pulse-driven gas sensor; (b) Extracted PTM-LT vs HT percentage; (c) Response time and recovery time of the sensor at different HT percentage; (d) Transient response of the sensor to 1 ppm NO2 (LT: 50℃, HT: 225℃); (e) PTM-LT and (f) Response/recovery time vs temperature; (g) Comparison of sensing properties in PTM-LT and isothermal LT.
(a) Responses under different concentration gradients and; (b) Car exhaust test under PTM mode (LT: 50℃, HT: 225℃) (X was the concentration of car exhaust).
(a) Schematic of the mechanism of Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 in air and NO2; (b) Simplified energy band diagram.

head.ce:title: High-performance NO2 gas sensor enabled by Fe, N co-doped GQDs modification and pulse-driven temperature modulation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics, Key Laboratory of Gas Sensors, College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun, Jilin Province 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics, Key Laboratory of Gas Sensors, College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics, Key Laboratory of Gas Sensors, College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun, Jilin Province, 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International Center of Future Science, Jilin University, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International Center of Future Science, Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International Center of Future Science, Jilin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen dioxide (NO2) is a typical reactive nitrogen species harmful to health and the environment. However, NO2 sensors still suffer from low sensitivity and poor response/recovery rates. Herein, 3D In2O3 assembled by stacked nanosheets were prepared and modified with N element-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) and Fe, N co-doped GQDs (Fe,N-GQDs), respectively. The results of NO2-sensing performance indicated that the optimal sample (0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3) showed a high response of 414.5 to 1 ppm NO2 and could detect as low as 10 ppb at 50℃, which was 1.3 and 4.0 times higher than that of 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3 and In2O3, respectively. The gas-sensing kinetics were analyzed by establishing gas adsorption/desorption isotherms. The enhanced NO2 sensing properties of 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 were mainly due to the enhanced active site accessibility of stacked nanosheets with open layer spaces, the heterointerface between Fe,N-GQDs and In2O3, and electrical modulation of Fe,N-GQDs. Additionally, A pulse-driving circuit was designed for a pulse temperature modulation (PTM) strategy to further boost NO2 detection. The sensor response is further enhanced by 1.7 times, and the response/recovery time is reduced by 42.5 %/60.1 %. The NO2 concentration in vehicle exhaust was determined using PTM mode with good spike recoveries. It validated the reliability of the sensor for the quantitative detection of NO2 in the real environment. This work provides insights into sensor design and has the potential to contribute to the development of novel sensing platforms for other gas pollutants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the significant improvement in industry and technology, environmental pollution is a growing public concern. Nitrogen dioxide (NO2) is a common dangerous gas in the environment. Given the severe environmental and health hazards posed by NO2 emissions from vehicle exhausts and industrial activities, there is an urgent need to develop a method for the precise detection of NO2 that ensures exceptional selectivity and low detection limit.

Metal oxide semiconductors (MOS)-based gas sensors have been used to detect NO2 primarily due to their simple fabrication, low power consumption and adjustable structures [1–5]. Indium oxide (In2O3), the n-type MOS material with a broad band-gap ranging from 3.55 to 3.75 eV and superior electrical conductivity, has garnered significant interest for its prospective use in gas-sensing applications. Many new attempts have been made to modify In2O3, of which morphological modulation and heterostructure engineering are both effective and simple ways to improve gas-sensing performance. Generally, the sensing activity of MOS can be improved by optimizing the surface adsorption/reaction kinetics by increasing the number of active sites. Various morphologies of In2O3, including nanowires [6], nanorods [7] and nanosheets [8,9] have been extensively studied. Two-dimensional (2D) layered MOS with a high surface-to-bulk ratio possess unique electronic structures but are prone to compact aggregation. Here was an effective strategy that constructing 2D nanosheets into well-defined 3D ordered architectures with open interlayer spaces not only possessed intrinsic characteristics of 2D nanostructures but also enhanced active sites accessibility and mass transport rate [5,10], which was favorable for the sensing properties. For example, Wei Yang et al. [5] constructed 2D In2O3 nanosheets into well-defined 3D architectures with a remarkable NO2 response. Apart from morphology control, constructing heterojunctions is always exploited because the formation of heterojunctions is available for depletion region enlargement and electron-hole pair separation [11]. Among various alternative materials for constructing heterojunctions, graphene-based materials have become attractive candidates due to their excellent electrical conductance [12], large specific surface area [13] and plentiful adsorption sites [14]. Particularly, reduced graphene oxide (rGO) has demonstrated potential as an effective material for gas detection, with its performance significantly affected by the interaction with gases being analyzed [15]. Up to now, several rGO-based composites have been applied for NO2 detection [16,17]. Regrettably, due to the large scale of conventional rGO sheets, it is unsuitable for modification in narrow interlayer spaces.

As an emerging member of the graphene family, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are 0D carbon nanomaterials with monolayer or multilayer graphene structures. GQDs possess excellent properties of graphene. In contrast to 2D graphene, GQDs offer a readily modifiable band gap ranging from 0 to 6 eV, attributed to their distinct quantum confinement and edge effects, along with adjustable electronic band structure [18]. The diminutive dimensions of GQDs contribute to an increased presence of oxygen-rich functional groups, establishing them as exceptionally reactive sites for chemical reactions [19]. Benefiting from its excellent properties, GQDs hold considerable promise for enhancing the gas detection capabilities of MOS sensors. [20,21]. More importantly, GQDs readily disperse into the open channels of stacked In2O3 nanosheet layers. Moreover, GQDs can be easily doped with other elements exhibiting tunable electrical properties, processing potential applications in gas sensors [21]. Nitrogen (N) as a dopant for GQDs provides more negative adsorption energy, thus enhancing NO2-sensing performance [20]. Beyond this, iron (Fe), a transition metal element with a vacant 3d orbital, contributes to the electron-transfer process [22,23]. Therefore, the effect of nitrogen and iron co-doped GQDs (Fe,N-GQDs) acting as an MOS surface modifier on NO2 sensing is worthy of investigation.

Herein, we synthesized 3D stacked In2O3 nanosheets and utilized heteroatomic doped GQDs (N-GQDs and Fe,N-GQDs) to decorate the surface of In2O3 (N-GQDs/In2O3 and Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3). The NO2-sensing performance results showed that the Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 sensor exhibited excellent selectivity and highly superior response than N-GQDs/In2O3 and In2O3. The optimized material with 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs content (0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3) exhibited improved sensing kinetics and could detect as low as 10 ppb. Furthermore, PTM was employed to further improve the gas-sensing performance of 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 sensor. This study provided a new method for highly sensitive detection of NO2 in the environment by constructing Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 sensors and applying PTM strategy.

0.293 g of InCl3·4 H2O and 1.456 g of CTAB were added to deionized water. Afterward, 0.304 g of thiourea was added into the above solution under stirring for 1 h. The mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated to 160°C for 14 h. The precursors were collected and washed, followed by drying at 80℃ for 12 h. To obtain the In2O3, these precursors were annealed in a muffle furnace at 600°C for 2 h. The typical synthesis process of Fe,N-GQDs is shown in Fig. 1a and supporting information [21].

To prepare Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 composites, 125 μL Fe,N-GQDs suspension (2 mg/mL) and 50 mg In2O3 were dispersed in deionized water and stirred for 30 min. The mixture was then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated for 4 h at a temperature of 150°C. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting suspension was collected, the obtained Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 contained 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs theoretically. Additionally, other contents of Fe,N-GQDs (0.1, 0.3 and 0.5 wt%) and N-GQDs/In2O3 material were prepared using the similar procedure (Fig. 1a).

The morphology of samples was confirmed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7200 F), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). The crystal structure was determined using X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Rigaku D/Max-2550, with a wavelength of λ=1.5418 Å). Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR) was conducted using the Nicolet In10 spectrometer, while Raman spectroscopy data was obtained by RENISHAWINVIA Micro-Raman spectrometer. The Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectra were recorded using the Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrometer. The surface area was determined using the Micromeritics Gemini VI 2390. The surface states were obtained using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermofisher escalab 250Xi). The work function (Wf) was assessed utilizing a Kelvin probe (KP 6500 Digital Kelvin Probe McAllister Technical Services Co., Ltd.). Mott Schottky plots were recorded utilizing the electrochemical workstation (CHI660E).

Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 powders were gently ground into a slurry and coated with a brush on the surface of Al2O3 ceramic tube. It was sintered for 2 h at 200℃. A Ni-Cr alloy heating wire was inserted into the ceramic tube. The coating material was aged at 60℃ for 96 h.

The response of the sensor was recorded by a static test system (Fluke 8864 A). The test bottle was 1 L. The response was defined as Rg/Ra for oxidizing gases such as NO2, and S=Ra/Rg for the reducing gases (Rg and Ra were the sensor resistances in the target gas and air). The response time and recovery time were defined as the sensor reaching 90 % of the total response when NO2 was adsorbed and desorbed.

Various gas cylinders including 100 ppm NO2, 500 ppm NO, 500 ppm NH3, 100 ppm SO2 and 500 ppm H2S were used as sources in the dry form. Liquid acetone and ethanol solutions were obtained from their analytical grade liquid reagents and evaporated in the test bottle. The formula for calculating the liquid volume required to configure gases was given in Eq. (1):
(1)
Q =
V × φ × M
22.4 × d × ρ
×
10
− 9
×
273 +
T
R
273 +
T
B
A detailed explanation of each term is given in supporting information.

The conventional isothermal model was displayed in Fig. 1b. Fig. 1c showed the schematic diagram of the PTM model. The PTM can be viewed as a rectangular heating waveform that incorporates varying high temperature (HT: 125–275°C) pulses and low temperature (LT: 50°C), which was applied on the gas sensor. The temperature period was set to 20 s. The NO2 response was defined as Rg-HT/Ra-HT at the high-temperature envelope and Rg-LT/Ra-LT at low-temperature envelope. The vehicle exhaust in the experiment was collected from the Energy and Power Experimental Lab, College of Automotive Engineering of Jilin University. 16 mL of vehicle exhaust was extracted for the NO2 concentration test.

N-GQDs and Fe,N-GQDs were initially synthesized via a bottom-up strategy using a hydrothermal method and then characterized by TEM, FTIR and UV–vis. TEM images illustrated that Fe,N-GQDs exhibited well-dispersed particles with an average size of approximately 2.7 nm (Fig. S1a).

The peak of FTIR spectra resulting from C-N exhibited a redshift after Fe doping since Fe was chelated with N [24] (Fig. S1b). Thus, the results of FTIR demonstrated the coordination between Fe and N-GQDs. In comparison to N-GQDs, a slight blue shift of UV–vis spectra was observed in the absorption spectrum of Fe,N-CQDs, which could be attributed to the enrichment of electron-rich Fe atoms in Fe,N-GQDs [23] (Fig. S1c). Furthermore, the band-gap was determined using the Tauc Plot (αhν)2=B(hν-Eg) [25]. The band-gap of N-GQDs and Fe,N-GQDs were 3.38 and 3.30 eV (Fig. S1d-e). This was consistent with the results in the references that doping heteroatoms in GQDs led to the reduction of band-gap [23,26]. Generally, the change of band-gap is mainly related to the charge redistribution caused by heteroatom doping [27]. The Fe, N co-doped GQDs might increase the electron density and result in a reduced band-gap.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of In2O3 and GQDs/In2O3 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of In2O3, N-GQDs/In2O3 and Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 was characterized by SEM. In2O3 was constructed with a large number of stacked nanosheets ( Fig. 2a). These nanosheets were approximately 2 µm in planar scale, coupling with 30 ∼ 40 nm thickness. The surface of each nanosheet was relatively smooth and composed of dense nanoparticles. There existed clear and rich interspaces between adjacent nanosheets. The perfect stacked nanosheet structure was maintained for the nanocomposites after depositing N-GQDs and Fe,N-GQDs (Fig. 2b-c). TEM and HRTEM were conducted to gain insight into the detailed morphology and phase composition of all materials (Fig. 2d-f). Fig. 2e-f showed that N-GQDs and Fe,N-GQDs (marked with white circles) were equably modified on the surface of In2O3. Besides, the lattice fringes observed in the sample exhibited interplanar spacings of 0.252 nm and 0.293 nm (insets of Fig. 2d-f). These spacings corresponded to the (400) and (222) planes of In2O3, respectively. Additionally, EDS elemental mapping showed that C, N, and Fe elements in Fe,N-GQDs were uniformly distributed throughout In2O3 nanosheets (Fig. 2g and Fig. S2), confirming the formation of heterogeneous structural interfaces.

The phase composition of the materials was determined by XRD ( Fig. 3a). All diffraction peaks of samples corresponded well to cubic In2O3 (JCPDS Card No.06–0416) without additional impurities. No diffraction peaks indicated the presence of Fe,N-GQDs, mainly due to the relatively low amount of Fe,N-GQDs.

The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms, along with their corresponding pore-size distribution curves were presented in Fig. 3b-c. The results indicated that the N-GQDs and Fe,N-GQDs had negligible effect on the specific area of the composites.

To confirm the effect of interface engineering by heteroatom-doped GQDs, the band-gaps (Eg) of samples were calculated using UV-Vis spectroscopy. The band gap of In2O3, 0.1 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3, 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 and 0.5 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 were 3.66, 3.54, 3.57 and 3.62 eV, respectively (Fig. 3d-e). Compared with In2O3, the redshift of the absorption edge for Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 was caused by the formation of p-n heterojunction on the interface [28,29]. Additionally, the band-gap of 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 (3.57 eV) was narrower than 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3 (3.62 eV), which might be due to the doping of Fe in N-GQDs further reduced the band-gap of Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3.

The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and their corresponding pore-size distribution curves were shown in Fig. 3b,c. The BET surface areas of In2O3, 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3 and 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 were 24.32, 25.49 and 25.08 m2g−1, respectively. The results indicated that the N-GQDs and Fe,N-GQDs had negligible effect on the specific area of the composites. Additionally, the pore sizes of samples were 18, 18 and 24 nm, proving the mesoporous structure of all samples.

Mott-Schottky tests were conducted to analyze the mechanism underlying the electrical conduction properties (Fig. 3f). The calculated ND (The calculation formula was given in supporting information) of In2O3, 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3 and 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 were 7.07×1018, 4.53×1018 and 4.13×1018 cm-3, respectively. The decrease of carrier density in 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3 and 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 indicated that an electron dissipative layer was formed at the interface of p-n heterojunction [28,29].

The XPS was performed to reveal the surface composition and chemical states of the samples. The Fe, N peak in the Fe,N-GQDs survey spectra confirmed that Fe, N atoms had been successfully doped in Fe,N-GQDs (Fig. S3). Fig. 4a also demonstrated In, N, C, O, N and Fe elements in 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3, confirming that Fe,N-GQDs were successfully modified on the surface of In2O3. The C 1 s spectra of Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 revealed four contributions: CC (284.6 eV), C-C (286.7 eV), CO (288.0 eV) and C-N (285.3 eV), confirming the existence of carboxyl functional groups, graphitic carbon and N doping [29] (Fig. 4b and Fig. S4a).

The In 3d state could be divided into In 3d5/2 (444.0 eV) and In 3d3/2 (451.6 eV) feature peaks. As shown in Fig. 4c and Fig. S4b, the In ions in the material consist of In+ ions and In3+ ions. As presented in Fig. 4d and Fig. S4c, the O 1 s spectra exhibited three Gaussian function peaks, corresponding to lattice oxygen (OL, ∼530.2 eV), defect sites with low oxygen coordination (OV, ∼531.3 eV) and chemisorbed oxygen (OC, ∼532.3 eV). The relative content of In+ ions and oxygen content was summarized in Table S1. Since In+ and OV can act as good electron donors and active sites, 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 with the highest In+ and OV content would facilitate oxygen molecule adsorption [30].

The N 1 s peaks of 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3 were centered at 399.4 eV, 400.1 eV, and 401.6 eV (Fig. 4e). These peaks correspond to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N. Compared with 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3, the slight shift of pyrrole N in 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 can be attributed to the formation of Fe-N bonds [22]. The Fe 2p peaks of Fe,N-GQDs and 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 were observed in Fig. 4f. Fe 2p3/2 (710.7 eV) and Fe 2p1/2 (724.4 eV) with a splitting value of 13.7 eV were attributed to spin-orbit peaks for Fe,N-GQDs. Compared with Fe,N-GQDs, the Fe 2p1/2 of 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 shifted about 4.3 eV to the lower energy, indicating the electron density around the iron atom increased. Notably, the peak intensity at 706.4 eV was much higher than that at 720.1 eV, which may originate from the interfering signal of In 3p1/3.

Herein, the sensing performance of In2O3, 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3, 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 sensors towards 1 ppm NO2 at 25–125℃ was measured ( Fig. 5a). The curves for all samples showed unimodal distribution, and the optimized working temperature was 50℃. The response of In2O3, 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3 and 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 sensor was 103.2, 325.9 and 414.5. Notably, the sensitivity of the 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 sensor was 1.3 and 4.0 times higher than that of 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3 and In2O3, respectively. Therefore, the results illustrated that modifying GQD with In2O3 effectively enhanced the sensitivity of the materials, and the co-doped GQDs might offer superior electronic properties compared with single heteroatom-doped GQDs [23]. The impact of different doping ratios of Fe,N-GQDs on the sensitivity of the composites was further investigated (Fig. 5b). It can be seen that 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 sensor had the highest response of 414.5 to 1 ppm NO2 at 50°C. We speculated that appropriate Fe,N-GQDs content (0.3 wt%) contributed to the formation of relatively homogeneous heterojunctions between Fe,N-GQDs and stacked In2O3 nanosheets, while more (0.5 wt%) Fe,N-GQDs doping lead to aggregation of Fe,N-GQDs, which was detrimental to the improved gas sensing performance.

The baseline resistance (Ra) showed a decreasing trend with increasing temperatures ranging from 25°C to 125°C (Fig. 5c). It could be seen that the Ra of the materials increased with the increase of Fe,N-GQDs content on the In2O3 surface. The observed phenomenon likely results from the extraction of more electrons from In2O3 due to the additional Fe,N-GQDs content, resulting in the formation of wider interfacial electron depletion layers and thus increasing the resistance of composites. It was worth noting that 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 had a greater resistance compared with 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3, which indicated the formation of a wider space charge layer at the interface of Fe,N-GQDs and In2O3, resulting in higher resistance.

The dynamic responses of all sensors were recorded from 0.01 to 10 ppm NO2 (Fig. 5d-h). The response process was carried out at 50°C, while a high-temperature pulse (150°C) refresh strategy was used to accelerate the recovery kinetics. Subsequently, the temperature was reverted to 50°C once the sensor’s resistance had completely recovered. Furthermore, even at 10 ppb NO2 concentration, the response of In2O3, 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3, 0.1 wt%, 0.3 wt% and 0.5 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 could still reach 1.11, 1.77, 1.10, 2.35 and 1.18, respectively (Fig. 5i).

The transient resistance was investigated to analyze the reaction kinetics. The corresponding gas-sensing kinetics can be analyzed using Eq. (2) [31]:
(2)
N
O
2
+
S
a d s
+
e
−
⟶
←
K − r
K r
N
O
2
−
Where Sads denoted the adsorption sites on the material surface, the kinetic rate constants Kr and K-r correspond to the adsorption and desorption processes of NO2 molecules. The derivation and relevant parameters are detailed in the supporting information.

Fig. 6a-c presented the response transient of conductance of In2O3, 0.3 w% N-GQDs/In2O3 and 0.3 w% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 at different NO2 concentrations. According to Eq. (3), Kr and K-r of In2O3, 0.3 w% N-GQDs/In2O3 and 0.3 w% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 NO2 sensor were obtained using the τres values obtained from the response curves.
(3)
τ
r e s
=
(
K
r
× C +
K
− r
)
− 1

The fitting curves relating the 1/τres to NO2 concentration are shown in Fig. 6d, the Kr (K-r) of In2O3, 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3 and 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 were 4.32×10-6 (1.73×10-3), 9.56×10-6 (4.78×10-4) and 1.21×10-5 (1.66×10-3). The kinetic rate constants indicated that the modification of Fe,N co-doped GQDs for stacked In2O3 can greatly improve the response and recovery kinetics.

Moreover, the response times (τres) of the sensors for different concentrations of NO2 fit Freundlich isotherm, and the equation can be given as Eq. (4) [32]
(4)
τ
r e s
=
τ
0
×
C
− β
(5)
I n
τ
r e s
= β × I n C + I n α

The value of β was related to the activation energy of the reaction [30]. The above equation can be rewritten as Eq. (5) (Fig. 6e). The obtained β values of In2O3, 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3 and 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 were 0.467, 0.667 and 0.743. Compared to In2O3 and 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3, the β value of 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 increased, indicating enhanced reaction's active energy after modification with Fe,N-GQDs.

To further understand the kinetic rate, an analysis regarding the response and recovery time was carried out. 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 showed faster response and recovery times than In2O3 and 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3 sensors (Fig. 6f-g). Taking the dynamic process of 1 ppm NO2 as an example, the response/recovery times of the Fe,N-GQDs modified In2O3 sensor were reduced by 40.7 %/46.8 % and 8.4 %/29.5 % compared with the stacked In2O3 and 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3 sensors, respectively. The faster response and recovery process could be attributed to the better electron transport pathway from Fe,N co-doped GQDs. Besides, appropriate amounts of Fe,N-GQDs could effectively modulate the response/recovery kinetics. The response/recovery time decreased with growing Fe,N-GQDs content up to 0.3 wt% and then increased with further increments of Fe,N-GQDs (Fig. 6h-i). In short, the 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 sensor showed the highest response (414.5) and the fastest response/recovery time to 1 ppm NO2.

The repeatability of the sensors was assessed by alternating in air and NO2 for five cycles. The 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 sensor exhibited high stability ( Fig. 7a). The sensor was also tested at 1 ppm NO2 every two days within a month (Fig. 7b). The results showed the response fluctuated within an acceptable range, indicating an outstanding long-term stability. Additionally, the 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 sensor response to 1 ppm NO2 at 20 % - 80 % relative humidity (RH) was evaluated (Fig. 7c). The sensor responses reduced along with the promoted RH, which might be ascribed that the water molecules occupied the active sites for NO2 adsorption. Notably, the moisture resistance of 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 was improved to some extent with the doping Fe,N-GQDs. Fig. 7d exhibited the cross responses of sensors to interfering gases. The reaction to other tested gases was insignificant compared to that of NO2, announcing the excellent NO2 selectivity of 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3-based sensor. Furthermore, to evaluate the consistency of the gas sensor, five batches of 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 sensors were constructed and tested to 1 ppm NO2 at 50℃. As displayed in Fig. S5, these sensors fluctuated within an acceptable range, further suggesting the good consistency of the device in NO2 detection.

Low temperatures favored more gas adsorption, while high temperatures were required for surface activation of sensing materials [33,34]. A PTM strategy was employed utilizing a circuit module ( Fig. 8a), decoupling the adsorption and surface activation processes to enhance the gas-sensing performance of 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 sensor. The optimal protocol of PTM, including high temperature duty percentage, the rational high and low operating temperature, was discussed. Fig. S6 showed the dynamic resistance variation of the 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 sensor to 1 ppm NO2 in PTM mode (LT: 50°C, HT: 225°C) with different HT duty cycles. The response for the PTM-LT of the sensor increased with the HT duty cycle up to 15 % and decreased with a further increase of the HT duty cycle (Fig. 8b). Additionally, the response/recovery time decreased significantly when the HT duty cycle was up to 15 % (Fig. 8c). Consequently, taking the results of response and response/recovery time into account, 15 % was selected as the optimum HT duty cycle in PTM mode (Fig. 8c-d).

The transient resistance of 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 sensor under PTM with HT pulse varied from 125 to 275°C was shown in Fig. 8e-f and Fig. S7. 225°C was selected as a reasonable PTM-HT mode by integrating both response and response/recovery times. Subsequently, 50℃ was selected for PTM-LT mode (Fig. S8). A comparison of sensor performance acquired by PTM-LT and conventional isothermal-LT was shown in Fig. 8g. The 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 sensor exhibited a 1.7 times higher response to 1 ppm NO2 under the optimal PTM-LT mode compared to that under the optimal isothermal-LT mode. The response/recovery time under PTM-LT was reduced by 42.5 %/60.1 % compared with the optimal isothermal-LT mode. The above results demonstrated the feasibility of PTM mode in improving the performance of as-prepared Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of NO2 in environmental vehicle exhaust
ce:section.ce:para: The PTM mode of 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 sensor was used to detect NO2 in vehicle exhaust, to evaluate its suitability for use in real-world environmental samples. The calibration curve was first established against the standard NO2. The response values of the sensor in the range of NO2 concentrations from 0.2 ppm to 3 ppm under PTM mode were given in Fig. 9a. Next, the NO2 concentration in vehicle exhaust was determined using the calibration curve (Fig. 9b). Then, a standard addition method was employed with 0.2 ppm, 0.5 ppm, and 1 ppm NO2. The spike recoveries were 109.6 %, 93.1 % and 104.1 %, respectively. The results were all within acceptable error limits, which further validated the reliability of the 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 sensor for the quantitative detection of NO2 in the real environment.

The sensing mechanism of In2O3-based gas sensors towards NO2 has been frequently discussed using a model of electron depletion layer (EDL) [35]. For In2O3 exposed to air, O2 molecules adsorbed on the material’s surface and ionized into O2 - by trapping electrons in the conduction band, thus forming an EDL. When the sensor was exposed to NO2 with strong electron affinity, the absorbed NO2 further captured electrons from In2O3, forming NO2 − or reacted with the chemisorbed oxygen atom O−, leading to the expansion of the EDL, an increase in the barrier, and the increase of the resistance change, which ultimately improved the NO2 response.
In comparison with In2O3, the enhancement in sensing performances for 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 sensor can be attributed to the following factors:

Firstly, stacked In2O3 nanosheets possessed rich nanopores and interlayer spaces, providing a better gas diffusion path for NO2. Compared with gas-insulated graphene sheets, Fe,N-GQDs were smaller in size, allowing NO2 molecules to reach the surface and interface of the sensing material easily, thus forming EDL on the surface of each nanosheet, which was conducive to the improvement of sensitivity [36]. Additionally, PTM mode decoupled gas adsorption and sensing layer activation processes, which was favorable for gas adsorption.

Secondly, the formation of heterointerfaces between Fe,N-GQDs and In2O3 was vital in enhancing NO2 response. The zero-dimensional morphology of Fe,N-GQDs provided an extensive interface for enhanced NO2-sensing properties. The energy band diagram was displayed in Fig. 10. The band gap of Fe,N-GQDs and In2O3 were 3.30 and 3.66 eV. The work functions of Fe,N-GQDs and In2O3 were 5.03 eV and 4.24 eV (Fig. S9). When materials came into contact, electrons transferred from In2O3 to Fe,N-GQDs until their Fermi levels were aligned. During this process, the energy band-bending occurred at the interface, forming the interface depletion layer. Thus, Fe,N-GQDs reduced the electron density on the surface of In2O3 and widened the thickness of EDL, resulting in a more significant change in resistance when exposed to NO2, thus enhancing the NO2 response.

Furthermore, Fe,N-GQDs could provide additional active sites for NO2 adsorption of 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3. The number of chemisorbed oxygen species (O2 -) was increased by the modification of Fe,N-GQDs, which promote the adsorption of NO2. Additionally, density functional theory (DFT) has also evidenced that the N-doped graphene materials possessed more negative adsorption energy for NO2, and nitro-doped graphene systems near the Fermi level (Ef) exhibited basic properties and tended to enhance the basicity of carbon materials, which was known to favor NO2 adsorption [37,38].

Moreover, the presence of Fe and N atoms in GQDs improved the electron transfer rate, thereby improving the conductivity and providing high mobility for the carrier in GQDs. Besides the perspective of sensing material, PTM-HT offered a high-temperature zone that was beneficial for electron activation. When NO2 adsorbed on the Fe,N-GQDs surface, the surface charge transfer rate between NO2 molecules and Fe,N-GQDs was enhanced, thus enhancing the NO2-sensing performance.

Herein, In2O3 assembled by stacked nanosheets were successfully constructed and modified with N-GQDs and Fe,N-GQDs via a facile hydrothermal method. The results of NO2-sensing performance indicated that the response of 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 sensor was 1.3 and 4.0 times higher than that of 0.3 wt% N-GQDs/In2O3 and In2O3, respectively. A series of Fe,N-GQDs modified In2O3 stacked nanosheets were successfully prepared. The optimal sample (0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3) exhibited an enhanced response (414.5) with a shortened response/recovery time toward 1 ppm NO2 at 50℃. It was concluded that such enhanced NO2-sensing performance was ascribed to the enhanced active site accessibility, the heterointerface between Fe,N-GQDs and In2O3 and the electrical modulation of Fe,N-GQDs. The NO2-sensing performance of 0.3 wt% Fe,N-GQDs/In2O3 sensor was further improved by PTM. The NO2 response (1 ppm) was improved from 414.5 to 715.5 and the response/recovery time was reduced under optimal PTM mode. Finally, due to the above features, we anticipate that this sensor system can be applied to a range of environmental sensing applications, such as monitoring NO2 from automobile exhaust and industrial emissions.

Jiayin Han: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Weirong Zhou: Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Dehao Kong: Visualization, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yubing Gao: Visualization, Methodology. Yuan Gao: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Yong Wang: Visualization, Methodology. Geyu Lu: Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (62171196, U23A20293), Natural Science Foundation of Jilin Province (20240101375JC), and Science and Technology Project of Jilin Provincial Department of Education (JJKH20231167KJ).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.136040.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced electronic properties of SnO(2) via electron transfer from graphene quantum dots for efficient perovskite solar cells
ACS Nano

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Xie, et al., Enhanced Electronic Properties of SnO(2) via Electron Transfer from Graphene Quantum Dots for Efficient Perovskite Solar Cells, ACS Nano. 11 (2017) 9176-9182. http://dx.doi.org/10.1021/acsnano.7b04070.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jiayin Han received her BS degree from Jilin University of China in 2020. Now she is a graduate student in the Electronics Science and Engineering department, Jilin University. Her current researches focus on the synthesis and application of graphene and semiconductor oxide, especially in gas sensor.

Weirong Zhou received his BE degree from the Electronic Science and Engineering department, Jilin University, China in 2021. Now, he is a graduate student and interested in the synthesis and characterization of the semiconducting functional materials and gas sensors.

Dehao Kong received his BE degree from Jilin University of China in 2019. Now he is a graduate student in the Electronics Science and Engineering department, Jilin University. His current researches focus on the preparation and application of mesoporous semiconductor oxide, especially in gas sensor.

Yubing Gao received her BS degree from the Optoelectronic information Science and Engineering in 2017. Now, she is a graduate student and interested in the synthesis and characterization of the semiconducting functional materials and gas sensors.

Yuan Gao received her Ph.D. degree from Department of Analytical Chemistry at Jilin University in 2012. Now she is a professor in Jilin University, China. Her current research is focus on the preparation and application of graphene and semiconductor oxide, especial in gas sensor and biosensor.

Yong Wang - received her BS degree from Jilin University of China. Now he is a graduate student in the Electronics Science and Engineering department, Jilin University. His current researches focus on the synthesis and application of semiconductor oxide, especially in gas sensor.

Geyu Lu received the BS degree in electronic sciences in 1985 and the MS degree in 1988 from Jilin University in China and the Dr. Eng. degree in 1998 from Kyushu University in Japan. Now he is a professor of Jilin University, China. His current research interests include the development of chemical sensors and the application of the function materials.
